note: Dev term of the day — shown by the hub banner (app.js): the list is consumed in order, one term per day the banner is actually seen (seen days are tracked in reading-state.json and synced across devices), so a day the site isn't opened doesn't skip a term — the walk pauses and resumes. Topped up in batches by .github/workflows/glossary.yml per skills/GLOSSARY.md. APPEND new terms to the END only — never reorder, remove, or rename existing entries, and never change start_date (reordering would silently change the sequence mid-walk; start_date anchors the runway check and the one-time seen-day seed). If top-ups lapse and the list runs out, the banner wraps back to the oldest terms rather than going dark.

terms.def: Short for repository: the folder of a project that git tracks — all the code plus the full history of every change ever made to it.
terms.eg: "I cloned the repo" = downloaded the project and its whole history.

terms.def: A saved snapshot of your changes, with a message describing them. A project's history is a chain of commits.
terms.eg: "Committed the fix" = the change is now recorded in the project's history.

terms.def: Push uploads your local commits to GitHub; pull downloads everyone else's latest commits to your machine.
terms.eg: "Pushed to main" = the changes are now up on GitHub for all to see.

terms.def: A parallel line of work in git, so you can change things freely without touching the main copy until the work is ready.
terms.eg: "I'm on a feature branch" = working on a side copy; main stays safe.

terms.def: Combining the work from one branch into another — typically a finished feature branch into main.
terms.eg: "Merged the PR" = that work is now part of the main codebase.

terms.def: A proposal on GitHub to merge one branch into another, where the changes are reviewed and discussed first. Despite the name, it's really about review.
terms.eg: "Open a PR" = put the change up for review before it joins main.

terms.def: The line-by-line difference between two versions of code: what was added (+) and what was removed (−). What you actually review in a PR.
terms.eg: "Check the diff" = look at exactly which lines changed.

terms.def: The primary branch of a repo — the current 'real' version that everything else eventually merges into. Older projects call it master.
terms.eg: "Never push straight to main" — changes should arrive via reviewed PRs.

terms.def: Copying an entire repo — code and history — from GitHub onto a machine so you can work on it locally.
terms.eg: "git clone" is usually step one of touching any project.

terms.def: Command-line interface: a program you drive by typing commands in a terminal rather than clicking buttons. Claude Code is one.
terms.eg: "Use the CLI" = do it by typing a command, not through an app's menus.

terms.def: The terminal is the text window; the shell (bash, zsh…) is the program inside it that reads your commands and runs them.
terms.eg: "Run this in your terminal" = type it at the prompt and press Enter.

terms.def: A small program, usually run from the terminal, that automates a task end to end.
terms.eg: "There's a build script" = one command does the whole build for you.

terms.def: Node's package manager: the tool (and huge online registry) JavaScript projects use to install the libraries they depend on.
terms.eg: "npm install" fetches every library a project needs in one go.

terms.def: Reusable code someone else wrote that your project pulls in, instead of writing that functionality from scratch.
terms.eg: "There's a library for that" — the developer's favourite sentence.

terms.def: Any external package your project needs to run. They're listed in a manifest file so anyone can install the exact same set.
terms.eg: "Update the dependencies" = pull in newer versions of those libraries.

terms.def: An auto-generated file (like package-lock.json) pinning the exact version of every dependency, so installs come out identical on every machine.
terms.eg: "Commit the lockfile" so your teammate's install matches yours byte-for-byte.

terms.def: Turning source code into its runnable or shippable form — compiling, bundling, minifying. Also the noun for the result.
terms.eg: "The build is broken" = that process currently fails, so nothing can ship.

terms.def: Translating human-written source code into a form the machine (or browser) can actually execute.
terms.eg: "It won't compile" = errors are stopping the translation step entirely.

terms.def: A tool that reads code without running it and flags style problems and likely bugs. Its complaints are 'lint errors'.
terms.eg: "CI failed on lint" = the style checker objected, not the tests.

terms.def: An automated check that one small piece of code (usually a single function) behaves as expected. Runs in milliseconds, by machine.
terms.eg: "Add a unit test for that" = pin the behaviour down so it can't silently break.

terms.def: A test that checks several parts working together — say, the app talking to a real database. Slower but more realistic than unit tests.
terms.eg: "Unit tests pass but integration fails" = the pieces work alone, not together.

terms.def: A quick 'does it even switch on?' check after a change or deploy — run the main flows and confirm nothing catches fire before testing deeper.
terms.eg: "Just smoke test it" = open the app, click through the basics, look for flames.

terms.def: When a change accidentally breaks something that used to work. The main reason automated tests exist.
terms.eg: "That caused a regression" = the new code broke an old feature.

terms.def: Continuous integration: a robot that automatically runs the tests, linter and build on every push or PR — the 'checks' on GitHub. Green tick = all passed.
terms.eg: "Wait for CI" = don't merge until the robot finishes its checks.

terms.def: Continuous delivery/deployment: the follow-on robot — once CI is green, the code ships to servers automatically.
terms.eg: "We have CI/CD" = merging to main is what deploys the site.

terms.def: Releasing code onto the servers (or app store, or website) where it actually runs for real users.
terms.eg: "Deployed to production" = it's live now.

terms.def: The live environment that real users touch — 'prod' for short. Its siblings: staging (the dress rehearsal copy) and dev (a developer's own machine).
terms.eg: "Don't test in prod" = don't experiment on the live system.

terms.def: A place code runs, with its own settings — dev, staging, production. Same code, different configuration.
terms.eg: "Works in dev, breaks in prod" — a classic, usually a config difference.

terms.def: A named setting (like an API key) handed to a program from outside the code, so secrets and config never get written into the source.
terms.eg: "Set the API key as an env var" = supply it at run time, don't hardcode it.

terms.def: Application programming interface: the defined way one program asks another for something — the menu a service lets other software order from.
terms.eg: "Call the weather API" = ask the weather service for data, program to program.

terms.def: One specific URL of an API that you can call — each endpoint does one job.
terms.eg: "/users/42 is the endpoint" for fetching user number 42.

terms.def: The most common API style: every thing gets a URL, and you use HTTP verbs on it — GET to read, POST to create, DELETE to remove.
terms.eg: "It's a REST API" = predictable URLs and verbs, no surprises.

terms.def: A reverse API call: you give a service a URL of yours, and it calls you when something happens — 'don't call us, we'll call you'.
terms.eg: "GitHub fires a webhook on every push" = it pings your URL each time code lands.

terms.def: The standard text format programs use to exchange structured data — nested {"key": "value"} pairs. Human-readable-ish, machine-perfect.
terms.eg: "The API returns JSON" = data arrives as those bracketed key–value pairs.

terms.def: A config file format that uses indentation instead of braces. Very common for CI pipelines — GitHub Actions files are YAML.
terms.eg: "Edit the workflow YAML" = change the .yml file that tells CI what to run.

terms.def: Your own machine, as a web address (127.0.0.1). Apps 'on localhost' are served only to your computer.
terms.eg: "Running on localhost:3000" = open that address in your browser to see it — nobody else can.

terms.def: A numbered door on a machine that one program listens on, so many services can share one computer.
terms.eg: "Port 3000 is already in use" = another program has claimed that door.

terms.def: A server is a program (or the machine running it) that waits for requests and answers them; the client is whoever asks — usually your browser.
terms.eg: "Client-side vs server-side" = does this run in the browser, or on the server?

terms.def: Frontend = what runs in the browser (the interface). Backend = what runs on the server (data and logic). Full-stack = someone doing both.
terms.eg: "It's a backend bug" = the server logic is wrong, not the buttons.

terms.def: A big library that dictates the structure of your app and calls your code — you fill in the blanks. React, Django and Rails are frameworks.
terms.eg: "Which framework?" is usually the first question about any web project.

terms.def: An unusual input or situation at the boundary of what the code handles — an empty list, a leap day, a name with an apostrophe. Where bugs live.
terms.eg: "Did you handle the edge cases?" = what happens when the input is weird?

terms.def: A bug where the outcome depends on which of two overlapping operations happens to finish first. Strikes intermittently, hated accordingly.
terms.eg: "Probably a race condition" — said of bugs that only appear sometimes.

terms.def: The error printout showing the chain of function calls that led to a crash. Read the top lines first — it's the map to the bug.
terms.eg: "Paste the stack trace" = show me the full error, not just 'it crashed'.

terms.def: An error a program 'throws' when it can't continue. Code can 'catch' it and recover; uncaught, it crashes the program.
terms.eg: "It threw an exception" = something went wrong enough to bail out.

terms.def: Messages a program writes as it runs ('user logged in', 'payment failed') so humans can reconstruct what happened. The first stop when debugging.
terms.eg: "Check the logs" — the developer equivalent of 'check the black box'.

terms.def: Hunting down why code misbehaves — reading logs, stepping through it line by line, adding print statements until the culprit shows.
terms.eg: "Spent all day debugging" = one day, one bug, possibly one character.

terms.def: Restructuring code to make it cleaner without changing what it does. Housekeeping, not features — behaviour stays identical.
terms.eg: "Just a refactor" = the code moved around, but nothing should work differently.

terms.def: The future cost of quick-and-dirty choices made today. Like real debt: fine in moderation, crippling interest if never paid down.
terms.eg: "We're carrying a lot of tech debt" = past shortcuts are slowing us down now.

terms.def: A value (URL, date, password…) written directly into the code instead of coming from config. Fast, inflexible, occasionally disastrous.
terms.eg: "The date was hardcoded" — why the demo broke the day after the demo.

terms.def: An urgent, minimal patch pushed straight to production to stop the bleeding, outside the normal release rhythm.
terms.eg: "Shipped a hotfix" = fixed the live fire first, tidy up later.

terms.def: Undoing a deploy: putting the previous working version back into production while you work out what broke.
terms.eg: "Roll it back" = restore the last good version, investigate calmly after.

terms.def: A stored copy of a result kept close by so it isn't recomputed or refetched every time. Fast, but can go stale.
terms.eg: "Clear your cache" = your browser is showing an old saved copy.

terms.def: A secret string that proves you're allowed to use an API — like a hotel keycard: grants access, can expire, can be revoked.
terms.eg: "Paste your GitHub token" = give the app its keycard to act on your behalf.

terms.def: Short for authentication (proving who you are — login) and authorisation (what you're allowed to do — permissions). Both get shortened to 'auth'.
terms.eg: "The auth is broken" = people can't log in, or can't reach what they should.

terms.def: The 'Sign in with Google/GitHub' protocol: lets an app act on your behalf at another service without ever seeing your password.
terms.eg: "We use OAuth" = you approve access once; no password changes hands.

terms.def: Scrambling data so only key-holders can read it — 'at rest' (stored) and 'in transit' (the HTTPS padlock).
terms.eg: "End-to-end encrypted" = not even the service in the middle can read it.

terms.def: A one-way fingerprint of data: the same input always gives the same short string, but you can't reverse it. How passwords are stored and changes detected.
terms.eg: "Passwords are hashed" = the site stores fingerprints, not the passwords themselves.

terms.def: The three-digit result of every web request: 2xx OK, 3xx redirect, 4xx your mistake (404 not found, 401 not logged in), 5xx the server's mistake.
terms.eg: "Getting a 500" = the server itself is erroring — not your fault.

terms.def: Giving up on a request after a set wait, rather than hanging forever. A clean failure beats an eternal spinner.
terms.eg: "The request timed out" = the other side didn't answer in time.

terms.def: A cap on how many requests you may make per minute/hour. Exceed it and you're told to slow down (HTTP 429).
terms.eg: "We hit the rate limit" = too many calls too fast; wait and retry.

terms.def: Code that kicks off a slow job (like a network call) and carries on, handling the result whenever it arrives — instead of standing still waiting.
terms.eg: "It's async" = the answer comes later; the app doesn't freeze meanwhile.

terms.def: JavaScript's IOU for an async result: an object that will eventually hold the answer, letting you chain 'then do this' onto it.
terms.eg: "The function returns a promise" = you get an IOU now, data later.

terms.def: Two meanings: the program that executes your code (Node is a JavaScript runtime), and 'at runtime' = while the program runs, as opposed to at build time.
terms.eg: "A runtime error" = it compiled fine but blew up while running.

terms.def: The runtime that lets JavaScript run outside a browser — on servers, laptops and build scripts. Comes with npm.
terms.eg: "It's a Node script" = JavaScript you run from the terminal, no browser involved.

terms.def: Code whose licence lets anyone read, use, modify and share it. Most of the modern software stack is built on it.
terms.eg: "It's open source" = the code is public; you can read exactly what it does.

terms.def: Your own copy of someone else's repo, made so you can change it independently — with the option to offer changes back.
terms.eg: "Fork it and experiment" = copy the project to your account and go wild.

terms.def: The lightweight text format of READMEs and chat: **bold**, # headings, - lists. Plain text that renders as formatted text.
terms.eg: "Write it in Markdown" — .md files are Markdown, including this site's docs.

terms.def: The front-page file of a repo (README.md) that tells a newcomer what the project is and how to run it.
terms.eg: "Check the README first" — the instruction manual, when someone bothered to write one.

terms.def: A list of files git should never track — build output, secrets, local config. Keeps junk and keys out of the repo.
terms.eg: "Add it to .gitignore" = make sure this file never gets committed.

terms.def: Semantic versioning: version numbers as MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH (e.g. 2.4.1), where a MAJOR bump warns 'this will break existing users'.
terms.eg: "It's a major version bump" = expect breaking changes when you upgrade.

terms.def: A change that stops existing code or setups working until their owners adapt. The thing changelogs shout about in capitals.
terms.eg: "BREAKING:" in release notes = read this before you upgrade.

terms.def: Still works, but officially on notice for removal. Stop using it in new code and migrate old code when you can.
terms.eg: "That API is deprecated" = it lives, but its days are numbered.

terms.def: The repetitive standard code every project needs before the interesting part starts. Usually copy-pasted or generated.
terms.eg: "Mostly boilerplate" = lots of lines, little substance — the ceremony, not the logic.

terms.def: Generated starter files and structure for a new project or feature — the frame you then build inside.
terms.eg: "Scaffold a new app" = one command lays out all the standard folders.

terms.def: Minimum viable product: the smallest version that's genuinely usable, shipped early so you learn from real use instead of guessing.
terms.eg: "Ship the MVP first" = cut everything non-essential, launch, then iterate.

terms.def: A rough build that answers exactly one question: can this work at all? Meant to be thrown away, frequently isn't.
terms.eg: "Just a POC" = don't judge the code quality; it exists to test the idea.

terms.def: Work in progress. A 'WIP PR' is shared early for feedback and visibly not ready to merge.
terms.eg: "Marked WIP" = look, comment, but don't merge it yet.

terms.def: 'Looks good to me' — the reviewer's stamp of approval on a pull request.
terms.eg: "LGTM, merging" = review passed; the change is going in.

terms.def: The chain of side-tasks you must finish before the real task: to fix the bug you must upgrade X, which needs Y, which requires Z…
terms.eg: "I've been yak shaving all morning" = three detours deep, original task untouched.

terms.def: Explaining your problem out loud — to a rubber duck, a colleague, or Claude — and solving it halfway through the explanation.
terms.eg: "You just rubber-ducked me" = thanks for listening; saying it aloud cracked it.

terms.def: Spending outsized energy debating trivial choices (the button's colour) because they're easier to argue about than the hard decisions.
terms.eg: "We're bikeshedding" = an hour on the name, five minutes on the architecture.

terms.def: 'Don't repeat yourself': keep one authoritative copy of each piece of logic, so a fix lands once instead of five times.
terms.eg: "Keep it DRY" = extract the duplicated code into one shared function.

terms.def: An unexplained literal value sitting in code — why 86400? Better as a named constant (SECONDS_PER_DAY) that documents itself.
terms.eg: "Replace that magic number" = give the mystery value a name.

terms.def: A pattern that isn't a bug but hints at deeper design trouble — like a single function running 400 lines.
terms.eg: "That's a code smell" = it works, but something about it says 'look closer'.

terms.def: Code that can never run, or is never called any more. Deleting it is a favour to every future reader.
terms.eg: "Just dead code" = safe to remove; nothing uses it.

terms.def: Safe to run twice: doing it again changes nothing more. 'Set volume to 5' is idempotent; 'turn volume up' is not.
terms.eg: "The script is idempotent" = re-running it after a failure can't double anything.

terms.def: Same inputs, same output, every time — the property that makes tests and builds trustworthy. The opposite is 'flaky'.
terms.eg: "The build is deterministic" = anyone building this code gets identical results.

terms.def: A test that passes and fails randomly without any code change — usually a hidden timing or ordering issue. Slowly erodes all trust in CI.
terms.eg: "Ignore it, it's flaky" — famous last words before a real failure is ignored too.

terms.def: A fake stand-in for something real (like a payment API) so tests run fast, offline and without side effects.
terms.eg: "Mock the API in tests" = don't hit the real service; use a scripted double.

terms.def: The percentage of code lines that tests actually exercise. A useful signal — and an easily gamed metric.
terms.eg: "Coverage dropped" = the new code arrived with fewer tests than the old.

terms.def: Test-driven development: write the failing test first, then write just enough code to make it pass. Design by proving it.
terms.eg: "We do TDD here" = red test → green test → tidy up, repeat.

terms.def: A test that drives the whole app like a real user — clicking, typing, navigating — to check the full journey works.
terms.eg: "The e2e suite takes 20 minutes" — thorough, realistic, and slow.

terms.def: Rolling out to a small percentage of users first. Like the canary in the coal mine, it feels trouble before everyone does.
terms.eg: "It's on canary" = 5% of users have it; the rest wait for the all-clear.

terms.def: An on/off switch in code that lets a feature be enabled remotely without redeploying — ship it dark, turn it on later.
terms.eg: "Behind a feature flag" = the code shipped, but users can't see it yet.

terms.def: Showing version A to some users and version B to others, then measuring which performs better. Deciding by data instead of debate.
terms.eg: "A/B test the headline" = let user behaviour pick the winner.

terms.def: The data an app phones home about itself — metrics, logs, traces — so its operators can see inside it while it runs in production.
terms.eg: "Check the telemetry" = what does the app itself say is happening out there?

terms.def: The delay before an answer starts arriving. Its sibling throughput is how much arrives per second — snappy vs high-volume.
terms.eg: "High latency" = each click takes ages to respond, even if the pipe is big.

terms.def: How gracefully a system handles growing load. 'Scale up' = a bigger machine; 'scale out' = more machines.
terms.eg: "Will it scale?" = fine for 100 users — what about 100,000?

terms.def: The traffic cop in front of multiple servers, spreading incoming requests across them so no single one drowns.
terms.eg: "Behind a load balancer" = many servers answer as one address.

terms.def: Content delivery network: copies of your static files parked in cities worldwide, so pages load from somewhere near the user.
terms.eg: "Serve it from a CDN" = images and scripts arrive from close by, fast.

terms.def: The internet's phone book: turns names like github.com into the numeric addresses machines actually use.
terms.eg: "It's always DNS" — the ops proverb for mysterious outages.

terms.def: The encryption layer behind the browser padlock. Certificates prove a site is who it claims and keep traffic private in transit.
terms.eg: "The cert expired" = the padlock broke and browsers now show scary warnings.

terms.def: A middleman that forwards your requests — used for caching, filtering, privacy, or corporate policy.
terms.eg: "Goes through a proxy" = traffic takes a supervised detour on its way out.

terms.def: Packaging an app plus its entire environment into a standard box that runs identically anywhere. Docker is the household name.
terms.eg: "Ship it as a container" = no more 'works on my machine' — the machine ships too.

terms.def: A whole simulated computer running inside another. Heavier than a container, but more isolated.
terms.eg: "Spin up a VM" = borrow a fresh computer that exists only in software.

terms.def: The orchestrator that runs and heals fleets of containers across many machines — restarts crashes, scales copies up and down. Famously complex; a.k.a. k8s.
terms.eg: "We run it on Kubernetes" = an automated fleet manager keeps the containers alive.

terms.def: Running code as small functions the cloud spins up on demand and bills per use. There are servers — you just never see them.
terms.eg: "Made it serverless" = no machines to manage; code runs when called.

terms.def: An isolated play area where code runs without being able to touch the wider system. Safety by containment.
terms.eg: "Runs in a sandbox" = even if it misbehaves, it can't reach anything important.

terms.def: One big repository holding many projects together, instead of one repo per project. Easier to share code, heavier to manage.
terms.eg: "It's a monorepo" = the app, the site and the tools all live in one repo.

terms.def: The classic scheduler that runs jobs at set times, configured with terse expressions like '0 2 * * *' (2am daily).
terms.eg: "A cron job runs nightly" — like the one that writes this site each morning.

terms.def: A long-running background program with no interface of its own, quietly doing work — servers, sync agents, schedulers.
terms.eg: "The daemon handles it" = a background process does that without being asked.

terms.def: The number a command reports when it finishes: 0 means success, anything else means failure. What scripts and CI check to decide what happens next.
terms.eg: "Exited with code 1" = the command failed; the pipeline stops here.

terms.def: The two output streams of every command: normal output and errors, kept separate so logs stay clean and pipeable.
terms.eg: "Errors go to stderr" = you can save the real output and still see complaints.

terms.def: The terminal command for searching text by pattern — and the verb for doing so.
terms.eg: "Grep the logs for that error" = search the log files for the message.

terms.def: Regular expression: a terse pattern language for matching text — \d{4} means 'four digits'. Powerful, and famously hard to read back.
terms.eg: "Match it with a regex" = describe the text shape once, find every instance.

terms.def: Filename wildcard patterns: *.js means every JavaScript file; ** means any folder depth.
terms.eg: "The config accepts globs" = you can say 'all .test.js files anywhere'.

terms.def: The extras after a command: arguments are its inputs; flags (like --verbose or -f) switch its behaviour.
terms.eg: "Run it with the --strict flag" = same command, stricter behaviour.

terms.def: Reading raw text into structured data a program can work with. The reverse — structure back to text — is serialising.
terms.eg: "Failed to parse the JSON" = the text was malformed and couldn't be read.

terms.def: The actual data carried inside a request or message, as distinct from its addressing and headers.
terms.eg: "Check the payload" = look at what was actually sent, not just where.

terms.def: The declared shape of data — which fields exist and what type each is — whether for a database table or a JSON document.
terms.eg: "That breaks the schema" = the data no longer matches the agreed shape.

terms.def: A scripted, versioned change to a database's structure, applied in order so every copy of the database evolves identically.
terms.eg: "Run the migrations" = bring this database up to the latest structure.

terms.def: A request for data, usually from a database. SQL is the classic language for writing them.
terms.eg: "That query is slow" = the data request itself is the bottleneck.

terms.def: An attack where user input gets executed as database commands because it wasn't kept separate from the query. Why input is 'sanitised'.
terms.eg: "Vulnerable to SQL injection" = a crafted form entry could read or wreck the database.

terms.def: Cross-site scripting: sneaking JavaScript into a page other people view. The reason sites 'escape' user-supplied content.
terms.eg: "Escape it or it's an XSS hole" = render user text as text, never as code.

terms.def: The browser rule controlling which other websites may call your API from their pages. Source of a famously baffling error message.
terms.eg: "Blocked by CORS" = the browser refused a cross-site request the API didn't allow.

terms.def: The browser's live object model of the page — the tree of elements that JavaScript reads and edits to make pages interactive.
terms.eg: "Update the DOM" = change what's on the page from code.

terms.def: Code registered to run when something happens — a click, a keypress, a page load. The heartbeat of interactive UI.
terms.eg: "Attach a click listener" = when this is clicked, run that function.

terms.def: A background script a website installs in your browser to cache itself and work offline — a key part of installable web apps (PWAs), including this one.
terms.eg: "The service worker serves it offline" = the app works in flight mode.

terms.def: A layout that adapts to the screen it's on — phone, tablet, desktop — rather than assuming one fixed size.
terms.eg: "Is it responsive?" = does it still look right on a phone?

terms.def: Accessibility ('a', 11 letters, 'y'): making software usable with screen readers, keyboards and low vision. Sibling: i18n = internationalisation.
terms.eg: "An a11y issue" = fine with a mouse and good eyes; broken without them.

terms.def: Code that back-fills a modern browser feature in old browsers that lack it, so you can write for the future today.
terms.eg: "Ship a polyfill" = old browsers get a stand-in for the missing feature.

terms.def: Stripping whitespace and shortening names in shipped code so it downloads smaller. Why production JavaScript looks like keyboard-mash.
terms.eg: "That's the minified bundle" = unreadable on purpose; the source looked normal.

terms.def: A tool that combines many source files into a few optimised ones for the browser — webpack, Vite and esbuild are bundlers.
terms.eg: "The bundler handles imports" = 300 files in, 2 files out.

terms.def: Translating source code from one language (or language version) to another — most famously TypeScript into JavaScript.
terms.eg: "It transpiles to JS" = browsers never see the original language.

terms.def: JavaScript plus declared types, checked before the code runs — catching whole categories of bugs at compile time. Compiles down to plain JavaScript.
terms.eg: "The codebase is TypeScript" = stricter JavaScript with a safety net.

terms.def: Verifying each value's kind (string, number, list…) matches what the code expects — catching 'passed a word where a number goes' before running.
terms.eg: "The type checker caught it" = a bug died at compile time, cheaply.

terms.def: Memory that keeps being claimed and never released, so the program slowly bloats until it crawls or crashes.
terms.eg: "Restart it — memory leak" = the app eats more RAM the longer it lives.

terms.def: The runtime automatically reclaiming memory nothing uses any more, so most languages never free memory by hand.
terms.eg: "GC pause" = the brief hiccup while the runtime takes out the trash.

terms.def: A process is a running program with its own memory; threads are parallel workers inside it that share that memory.
terms.eg: "Kill the process" = stop that running program entirely.

terms.def: An operation that completes entirely or not at all — no observable half-done state, even if things fail midway.
terms.eg: "The write is atomic" = you'll see the old data or the new, never a mangle.

terms.def: The plan B when a component fails: do something reduced-but-useful instead of collapsing. The art is 'graceful degradation'.
terms.eg: "Falls back to cached data" = if the network dies, show the last good copy.

terms.def: On failure, trying again after increasing waits — 2s, 4s, 8s — so a struggling service gets breathing room instead of a hammering.
terms.eg: "Retries with exponential backoff" = patient, polite persistence.

terms.def: Direction of dependency flow: upstream is the source you depend on (the original repo, the earlier stage); downstream is whoever depends on you.
terms.eg: "Fixed upstream" = the original project fixed it; we inherit it on update.

terms.def: A file produced by a build or CI run — the compiled app, a test report, an installer. The output worth keeping.
terms.eg: "Download the build artifact" = grab the file CI produced.

terms.def: A chain of automated stages code passes through — build, then test, then deploy. One red stage stops the line.
terms.eg: "The pipeline is red" = a stage failed; nothing ships until it's green.

terms.def: Any credential — key, token, password — that must stay out of code and repos. CI systems provide locked stores for them.
terms.eg: "Add it as a repo secret" = store the key in GitHub's vault, not in a file.

terms.def: Secure shell: encrypted terminal access to remote machines. SSH key pairs also replace passwords for talking to GitHub.
terms.eg: "SSH into the server" = open a secure terminal on a machine elsewhere.

terms.def: A fingerprint (hash) of a file, used to verify it wasn't corrupted or tampered with on the way to you.
terms.eg: "Verify the checksum" = confirm the download is byte-for-byte the real thing.

terms.def: CI shorthand: green = every check passed; red = something failed and merging is blocked. 'Breaking the build' makes it red for everyone.
terms.eg: "Keep the build green" = never merge something that fails the checks.

terms.def: Git replaying your branch's commits on top of the latest main, keeping history in one clean line — the tidier, touchier sibling of merging.
terms.eg: "Rebase onto main" = pretend you started from today's code all along.

terms.def: When git can't auto-combine two edits to the same lines and a human must choose which wins. The classic PR speed bump.
terms.eg: "It has conflicts" = someone must hand-pick between the competing edits.

terms.def: Git's pocket for uncommitted changes: whisk them out of the way, do something else, then pop them back.
terms.eg: "Stash it and switch branches" = park the half-done work, no commit needed.

terms.def: Copying one specific commit onto another branch without bringing its whole branch along.
terms.eg: "Cherry-pick the fix onto the release" = just that one change, nothing else.

terms.def: Shows who last touched each line of a file and in which commit. For archaeology — not accusation. Mostly.
terms.eg: "Blame says it changed in March" = that line's history names the commit.

terms.def: Extra working folders attached to one repo, so two branches can be checked out side by side at once. Claude Code uses them to isolate agents.
terms.eg: "Each agent gets a worktree" = parallel work, zero risk of stepping on each other.

terms.def: Modifying someone else's code at runtime from the outside, without changing its source. Powerful, fragile, frowned upon.
terms.eg: "We monkey-patched the library" = it works, and nobody is proud of it.

terms.def: A thin layer of code that adapts one interface to another so mismatched pieces can work together.
terms.eg: "A shim around the old API" = new code talks through a small adapter.

terms.def: Using your own product day-to-day ('eating your own dog food') so you hit its problems before your users do.
terms.eg: "We dogfood the beta" = the team lives on the unreleased version.

terms.def: How many people could be hit by a bus before nobody understands the system. A bus factor of one is a warning, not a flex.
terms.eg: "The bus factor on that service is one" = exactly one person knows how it works.

terms.def: The blameless write-up after an incident: what happened, why, and what will prevent a repeat. Focused on causes, not culprits.
terms.eg: "Read the post-mortem" = the honest story of the outage, minus finger-pointing.

terms.def: Being the designated person paged when production breaks — nights and weekends included. Rotates through the team.
terms.eg: "I'm on-call this week" = if it breaks at 3am, my phone screams.

terms.def: A bug that vanishes the moment you try to observe it — add a log line or attach a debugger, and it hides. Named for the uncertainty principle.
terms.eg: "Classic heisenbug" = crashes in production, refuses to crash while watched.

terms.def: Landing exactly one away from the right count — looping once too many, starting at 1 instead of 0. Tiny, ancient, everywhere.
terms.eg: The two hardest problems: naming, caching, and off-by-one errors.

terms.def: The value that means 'nothing here'. Forgetting to handle it causes some of computing's most common crashes — its inventor calls it the billion-dollar mistake.
terms.eg: "It was null" = the code expected a value and found an empty slot.

terms.def: Code that breaks the language's grammar rules — a missing bracket or comma — so it won't even start. The compiler tells you where. Roughly.
terms.eg: "Just a syntax error" = the code equivalent of a typo.

terms.def: One specific check a linter enforces ('no unused variables'). Teams enable, disable and argue about them individually.
terms.eg: "Disable that rule for this line" = we're breaking it here on purpose.

terms.def: A tool that rewrites code into one canonical style — indentation, quotes, line breaks — ending all formatting debates by decree.
terms.eg: "Run the formatter" = the style argument is over; the tool decides.

terms.def: A second developer reading your changes before they merge — catching bugs, sharing knowledge, keeping the codebase coherent.
terms.eg: "It's in review" = written and waiting on a colleague's eyes.

terms.def: Two developers, one screen: one types while the other thinks ahead and catches mistakes. Roles swap often.
terms.eg: "Let's pair on it" = work through the problem together at one keyboard.

terms.def: A fixed work cycle (usually two weeks) in agile teams: plan the batch, build it, demo it, repeat.
terms.eg: "Next sprint" = not now — it's queued for the next two-week cycle.

terms.def: The ordered to-do list of everything a team might build, from urgent fixes to someday-dreams. Groomed regularly, finished never.
terms.eg: "Add it to the backlog" = a polite place for ideas to wait their turn.

terms.def: One tracked unit of work in a tool like Jira or GitHub Issues — a bug report, a feature request, a chore. Referenced by number.
terms.eg: "File a ticket" = write it down properly so it gets prioritised and tracked.

terms.def: Greenfield = building brand new with no legacy constraints. Brownfield = building within an existing system's rules and rubble.
terms.eg: "A rare greenfield project" = blank page, no legacy code to appease.

terms.def: Old code the business still depends on but nobody fully understands or dares touch. Often the most profitable code in the building.
terms.eg: "It's legacy" = it works, don't breathe on it.

terms.def: A time-boxed throwaway experiment to answer a question — 'is this feasible?' — before committing to build it properly.
terms.eg: "Do a one-day spike" = investigate quickly, report back, throw the code away.

terms.def: The trivial detail everyone has opinions on. From Parkinson's law of triviality: a committee approves a nuclear plant in minutes, then debates the bike shed's colour for hours.
terms.eg: "That's bikeshed territory" = we're deep in opinions, far from consequences.

terms.def: Tuning code for speed before knowing where it's actually slow — 'the root of all evil' per Donald Knuth. Measure first, optimise second.
terms.eg: "That's premature optimisation" = make it correct first; profile before tuning.

terms.def: 'You aren't gonna need it': don't build flexibility for imagined future needs — build what's needed now. Most anticipated futures never arrive.
terms.eg: "YAGNI" — said while deleting the config option nobody asked for.

terms.def: The flow where everything goes right — valid input, network up, user cooperative. Easy to build; the sad paths are the real work.
terms.eg: "Only the happy path works" = one wrong input and it all falls over.

terms.def: A program finishing its in-flight work and tidying up before exiting, instead of being yanked mid-task.
terms.eg: "Handle SIGTERM gracefully" = when asked to stop, land the plane first.

terms.def: The dev-mode trick where saving a file updates the running app instantly, no restart — keeping your place and your flow.
terms.eg: "Hot reload picked it up" = the change appeared the moment you hit save.

terms.def: Read-eval-print loop: an interactive prompt that runs each line as you type it. Perfect for poking at a language or library.
terms.eg: "Try it in the REPL" = experiment live instead of writing a whole file.

terms.def: Software development kit: a bundle of libraries, tools and docs a platform ships so developers can build on it comfortably.
terms.eg: "Use the official SDK" = the vendor's ready-made toolkit for their API.

terms.def: Object-relational mapper: a library that lets you use the database through normal code objects instead of writing raw SQL.
terms.eg: "The ORM generates the SQL" = you write app code; it writes the queries.

terms.def: An API style where the client sends one query describing exactly the data shape it wants, instead of calling several fixed REST endpoints.
terms.eg: "The API is GraphQL" = ask for precisely the fields you need, in one round trip.

terms.def: A persistent two-way connection between browser and server, letting either side push messages instantly — how live chat and tickers work.
terms.eg: "Uses WebSockets" = the server can push updates without being asked each time.

terms.def: A cookie is a small token the browser sends back with every request; a session is the server-side memory of who you are that it unlocks.
terms.eg: "Session expired" = the server forgot you on purpose; log in again.

terms.def: A small key-value store inside the browser where a site can keep data on your device between visits — like this app's reading state.
terms.eg: "Saved in localStorage" = it lives in your browser, not on a server.

terms.def: Progressive web app: a website that installs like an app — home-screen icon, offline support, its own window. This reading app is one.
terms.eg: "Install it as a PWA" = add the site to your home screen; it behaves like an app.

terms.def: A branch created for one feature or fix, merged via PR when done, then deleted. The standard unit of team workflow.
terms.eg: "Cut a feature branch" = start the change on its own safe copy.

terms.def: Compressing a branch's many messy commits into one tidy commit at merge time, keeping main's history readable.
terms.eg: "Squash and merge" = fifteen 'fix typo' commits arrive as one clean change.

terms.def: A tag is a permanent named bookmark on one commit (v2.1.0); a release wraps it with notes and downloadable artifacts.
terms.eg: "Tagged v3.0" = that exact commit is forever the version 3.0.

terms.def: The file listing what changed in each released version — features, fixes, and the breaking changes you must read before upgrading.
terms.eg: "Check the changelog" = see what's new (and what breaks) since your version.

terms.def: Copying a dependency's source directly into your repo instead of installing it — trading auto-updates for total control.
terms.eg: "We vendored the library" = frozen copy in-repo; updates are now manual.

terms.def: Everything your software pulls in from outside — packages, their packages, build tools. An attack there poisons every project downstream.
terms.eg: "A supply chain attack" = the library you trusted shipped something hostile.

terms.def: A vulnerability attackers know about before defenders do — the vendor has had zero days to fix it when exploitation starts.
terms.eg: "Patched a zero-day" = the hole was being exploited before the fix existed.

terms.def: A numbered public record of a security vulnerability (CVE-2024-12345), so everyone can refer to exactly the same hole.
terms.eg: "Does that CVE affect us?" = is our version of the library the vulnerable one?

terms.def: Grant every person and program only the access it truly needs — so a compromise of one thing can't reach everything.
terms.eg: "Scope the token to one repo" — least privilege applied to API keys.

terms.def: Two-factor (multi-factor) authentication: login requires something you know (password) plus something you have (phone, key) — so a stolen password alone isn't enough.
terms.eg: "Enable 2FA on GitHub" = a stolen password alone can't take the account.
